question: A patient underwent abdominal CT for flank pain; renal calculi were confirmed and passed with conservative management. The CT also demonstrated an incidental finding (see image). Based on the imaging, which statement best describes the likelihood that the incidental finding reflects a hereditary condition that would substantially increase risk to first‑degree offspring?

options.A: High likelihood of autosomal dominant transmission
options.C: X‑linked inheritance risk for male offspring

ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the image: an axial contrast CT through the upper abdomen. The pancreas appears atrophic with multiple small high‑attenuation foci within the expected pancreatic parenchyma and along the ductal region; the arrow points to a dense focus in the region of the pancreatic duct consistent with a ductal stone. These findings are those of chronic pancreatitis (parenchymal atrophy with intraductal/intraparenchymal calcifications and a ductal stone).

Next, I relate what I see to the question, which asks whether this incidental imaging finding likely reflects a hereditary condition that would substantially increase risk to first‑degree offspring. The image demonstrates structural chronic changes but does not provide any information about family history, genetic testing, or other data required to establish an inherited syndrome.

Then, I consider how the answer choices align with what can be concluded from imaging alone. There is no imaging feature here that specifically indicates a particular mode of genetic transmission (autosomal dominant, X‑linked, mitochondrial, or autosomal recessive). Because the CT shows chronic pancreatitic change but gives no evidence of a genetic syndrome or familial pattern, one cannot infer a high likelihood of autosomal dominant or other inherited transmission from the scan alone.

Finally, tying these observations to the available options: the most supportable statement based solely on the imaging is that there is a low probability of hereditary transmission being indicated by these incidental findings. Therefore the best choice is option B.
caption: CT abdomen showing pancreatic atrophy with multiple sub-centimeter calcifications consistent with severe chronic pancreatitis: arrow points to dilated pancreatic duct with stones in it.
sub_caption: CT abdomen showing pancreatic atrophy with multiple sub-centimeter calcifications consistent with severe chronic pancreatitis: arrow points to dilated pancreatic duct with stones in it.
image_context: A 21-year-old male was referred to the gastroenterology clinic for incidental findings of severe chronic pancreatitis on cross-sectional imaging but no associated symptoms of pancreatitis. He had presented to the emergency department for abdominal pain consistent with prior episodes of renal colic. Computed tomography scan confirmed the presence of renal calculi that passed with conservative management. An incidental finding on the imaging showed marked pancreatic atrophy with multiple sub-centimeter calcifications consistent with severe chronic pancreatitis (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="PAMJ-28-110-g001">Figure 1</xref>). Remarkably, he did not report having any symptoms associated with pancreatic insufficiency including epigastric pain, vomiting, steatorrhea and weight loss. He denied having any prior episodes of acute pancreatitis and had no history of alcohol use. His laboratory tests were all within normal range, including complete blood count, liver function tests, amylase and lipase. His family history was significant as his father had an isolated episode of pancreatitis which required abdominal surgery. Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) showed severe parenchymal atrophy and pancreatic ductal stones with ductal dilation to 9mm (). Remarkably, he did not report having any symptoms associated with pancreatic insufficiency including epigastric pain, vomiting, steatorrhea and weight loss. He denied having any prior episodes of acute pancreatitis and had no history of alcohol use. His laboratory tests were all within normal range, including complete blood count, liver function tests, amylase and lipase. His family history was significant as his father had an isolated episode of pancreatitis which required abdominal surgery. Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) showed severe parenchymal atrophy and pancreatic ductal stones with ductal dilation to 9mm (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="PAMJ-28-110-g002">Figure 2</xref>). Due to his young age and atypical presentation, he was referred for genetic testing and counselling. He underwent genetic testing for various genes known to cause hereditary pancreatitis: CASR, CFTR, CTRC, PRSS1, SPINK1. He tested positive for homozygous variant of SPINK1 (N34S) mutation. Reflex testing of at-risk relatives confirmed that both of his parents were heterozygous carriers of the same SPINK1 (N34S) mutation and a review of their extended pedigree revealed that they were distant cousins. Given his asymptomatic clinical course, he remains under close follow-up without requiring any specific treatment for pancreatic insufficiency. Due to the severity of pancreatitis noted on imaging and long expected lifespan, he was counselled about the increased risk of pancreatic malignancy and is undergoing regular cross-sectional imaging for the detection and prevention of cancer.). Reflex testing of at-risk relatives confirmed that both of his parents were heterozygous carriers of the same SPINK1 (N34S) mutation and a review of their extended pedigree revealed that they were distant cousins. Given his asymptomatic clinical course, he remains under close follow-up without requiring any specific treatment for pancreatic insufficiency. Due to the severity of pancreatitis noted on imaging and long expected lifespan, he was counselled about the increased risk of pancreatic malignancy and is undergoing regular cross-sectional imaging for the detection and prevention of cancer.
relevant_image_context: Computed tomography scan confirmed the presence of renal calculi that passed with conservative management. An incidental finding on the imaging showed marked pancreatic atrophy with multiple sub-centimeter calcifications consistent with severe chronic pancreatitis.
